section.title: Justice and the Three Feasts
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes the Book of the Covenant's case law with two further movements: courtroom integrity that refuses to bend for either the powerful or the poor, and a calendar of rest and feasting that structures Israel's whole year around trust in God's provision.
section.thesis: The LORD demands impartial justice that neither follows the crowd nor favors the poor out of misplaced sympathy, commands a sabbatical year and weekly Sabbath rest for the sake of the poor and the land, and fixes three annual feasts that bring all Israel's males before Him in thanksgiving for His provision.
section.frameNote: Three movements: laws of courtroom and neighborly integrity, refusing false witness, bribery, and partiality in either direction; the sabbatical year and weekly Sabbath for land, servant, and animal alike; and the three annual pilgrimage feasts that anchor Israel's calendar in gratitude.

units.label: Impartial justice
units.note: neither crowd nor bribe nor favoritism

verses.text: “You shall not spread a false report. Do not join the wicked by being a malicious witness.
You shall not follow the crowd in wrongdoing. When you testify in a lawsuit, do not pervert justice by siding with the crowd.
And do not show favoritism to a poor man in his lawsuit.
If you encounter your enemy's stray ox or donkey, you must return it to him.
If you see the donkey of one who hates you fallen under its load, do not leave it there; you must help him with it.
You shall not deny {{justice|justice}} to the poor in their lawsuits.
Stay far away from a false accusation. Do not kill the innocent or the just, for I will not acquit the guilty.
Do not accept a bribe, for a bribe blinds those who see and twists the words of the righteous.
Do not oppress a foreign resident, since you yourselves know how it feels to be foreigners; for you were foreigners in the land of Egypt.

units.label: The sabbatical year and Sabbath day
units.note: rest for the land, the poor, and the servant

verses.text: For six years you are to sow your land and gather its produce,

but in the {{seventh|seventh}} year you must let it rest and lie fallow, so that the poor among your people may eat from the field and the wild animals may consume what they leave. Do the same with your vineyard and olive grove.

For six days you are to do your work, but on the seventh day you must cease, so that your ox and your donkey may rest and the son of your maidservant may be refreshed, as well as the foreign resident.
Pay close attention to everything I have said to you. You must not invoke the names of other gods; they must not be heard on your lips.

units.label: Three annual feasts
units.note: no one appears before Me empty-handed

verses.text: Three times a year you are to celebrate a {{feast|feast}} to Me.

You are to keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread as I commanded you: At the appointed time in the month of Abib you are to eat unleavened bread for seven days, because that was the month you came out of Egypt. No one may appear before Me empty-handed.

You are also to keep the Feast of Harvest with the firstfruits of the produce from what you sow in the field. 
And keep the Feast of Ingathering at the end of the year, when you gather your produce from the field.
Three times a year all your males are to appear before the Lord GOD.
You must not offer the blood of My sacrifices with anything leavened, nor may the fat of My feast remain until morning.

Bring the best of the firstfruits of your soil to the house of the LORD your God. 
You must not cook a young goat in its mother's milk.

justice.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of a legal case. This chapter insists mishpat must never bend, not toward the powerful through bribery, not toward the poor through misplaced sympathy, but must follow truth alone.

seventh.gloss: Release, the letting-go of the land every seventh year. The word shares its root with the release of debts later commanded in Deuteronomy 15, tying agricultural rest to a wider pattern of periodic release built into Israel's calendar.

feast.gloss: A pilgrimage feast, a festival marked by joyful gathering and travel to worship. Three times a year Israel's men were to leave their fields and travel to appear before the LORD, structuring the whole agricultural calendar around gratitude.

basic.context: This chapter closes Israel's early case law with instructions for honest courts, refusing bribes and favoritism in either direction, then moves into rhythms of rest, a sabbatical year for the land and a weekly Sabbath, and three annual feasts that bring the whole nation together to thank God for His provision.
basic.hermeneutics: These are specific instructions for Israel's ancient courts, agricultural calendar, and worship life under the old covenant. They are not direct legislation for modern courts or farming practices, but they reveal enduring principles: impartial justice, rest built into work, and gratitude structured into the rhythm of life, all fulfilled and carried forward under the new covenant.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's mishpat, justice, must never bend for bribe or crowd or misplaced sympathy for the poor; it must simply be true. The shemittah, the seventh-year release of the land, builds rest for the poor and the earth itself into the calendar. And three times a year Israel gathers for a chag, a joyful pilgrimage feast, before the LORD.
basic.typology: The Feast of Unleavened Bread, the Feast of Harvest, and the Feast of Ingathering trace the whole shape of redemption, deliverance, firstfruits, and final harvest, a pattern the New Testament reads as fulfilled in Christ's death, resurrection, and the final ingathering of all nations.
basic.christ: Paul calls Christ our Passover lamb and describes His resurrection as the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, 1 Corinthians 15:20, directly picking up the imagery of these very feasts; and the command against cooking a young goat in its mother's milk has long been read as protecting the boundary between life-giving nourishment and death, a boundary Christ crosses only to give life, never to bring death.
basic.god: God cares as much about courtroom integrity as He does about worship, refusing to let either the powerful or the poor manipulate justice, and He builds mercy and rest directly into Israel's economic calendar, insisting that even the land and the animals share in Sabbath rest. His holiness and His practical compassion are inseparable.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 calls Christ's resurrection the firstfruits, echoing the Feast of Harvest. Leviticus 25:1-7 expands the sabbatical year into the fuller Sabbath and Jubilee system. James 2:1-9 warns against partiality in judgment, echoing this chapter's insistence on impartial justice.
basic.oneStory: God insists on justice that bends for no one and builds rest and gratitude into the very rhythm of Israel's year. This pattern of impartial justice, rest, and thanksgiving before God carries through the whole Bible and finds its fulfillment in Christ, our Passover and our firstfruits, who secures the final, great ingathering of every nation.

context.literary: The chapter moves from courtroom ethics, refusing false witness, bribery, and favoritism, into agricultural and calendrical law, the sabbatical year, the weekly Sabbath, and the three pilgrimage feasts, closing the Book of the Covenant that began at 20:22 with a comprehensive vision of a just and grateful society.
context.historical: These pilgrimage feasts, Unleavened Bread, Harvest, or Weeks, and Ingathering, or Booths, structured Israel's entire agricultural year around specific acts of national worship, requiring every man to travel to the place of worship three times annually, a significant commitment of time and resources.
context.canonical: The concern for impartial justice recurs throughout the Prophets and Wisdom literature, while the three feasts, later called Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles, become the framework the New Testament uses to describe Christ's death, the giving of the Spirit, and the final harvest of the nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish courts that judge purely on truth rather than social pressure or bribery, and to build regular, structured rest and gratitude into Israel's agricultural and religious calendar as an expression of trust in God's provision.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes specific civil justice standards and an agricultural, festival calendar for ancient Israel under the old covenant; it does not directly mandate a sabbatical land-fallow system or pilgrimage feasts for believers today. Its enduring principles, uncompromising impartial justice and rhythms of rest and gratitude, are affirmed and reissued under the new covenant, without requiring the specific agrarian and festival forms given to Israel.

debates.question: Does verse 3's warning against favoring the poor in a lawsuit conflict with the Bible's frequent concern for the poor elsewhere?

views.label: No conflict, different contexts
views.case: Elsewhere Scripture commands generosity, care, and justice for the poor precisely because they are otherwise vulnerable to exploitation, Exodus 22:21-27; this verse addresses a courtroom specifically, warning that even good sympathy must not corrupt a judge's factual determination of guilt or innocence.

views.label: A necessary balancing corrective
views.case: Some read this verse as a deliberate check against a well-intentioned but real danger, that judges moved by compassion might excuse actual wrongdoing by a poor litigant, undermining the very justice system that protects the poor in the first place.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree: true justice requires impartiality toward all litigants regardless of social status, which is itself what ultimately protects the poor from arbitrary rulings in either direction.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's courts must render mishpat, true justice, refusing bribe, crowd pressure, or misplaced sympathy alike. The land itself observes shemittah, release, every seventh year, built into the calendar for the sake of the poor and the wild animals. And Israel's whole year turns on three occasions of chag, joyful pilgrimage feasting, gathering the nation before the LORD in gratitude.

typology.type: The Feast of Unleavened Bread, Feast of Harvest, Feast of Ingathering
typology.fulfillment: Traced by the New Testament into Christ's death as the Passover lamb, His resurrection as firstfruits, and the final ingathering of the nations, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23, Revelation 7:9.

typology.type: The sabbatical year of rest for land and poor
typology.fulfillment: A pattern of grace-based rest fulfilled in the greater Sabbath rest believers find in Christ, Hebrews 4:9-10.

study.christ: Paul explicitly draws on this festival calendar, calling Christ our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us, 1 Corinthians 5:7, and His resurrection the firstfruits of the greater harvest to come, 1 Corinthians 15:20. The three feasts that once structured Israel's year around remembered deliverance, present provision, and future harvest now find their center in Christ's finished work and His promised return, when the final ingathering of every nation will be complete.
study.god: God cares intensely about courtroom integrity, refusing to let either wealth or sympathy corrupt a verdict, and He builds mercy directly into Israel's economic structures, giving rest to land, servant, and animal alike. His justice never trades away truth for compassion, yet His compassion is woven into the very fabric of the calendar He gives His people.

crossReferences.note: Christ's resurrection as firstfruits directly echoes the Feast of Harvest introduced here.
Christ, our Passover lamb, fulfills the Feast of Unleavened Bread's deliverance theme.
Warns against partiality in judgment, directly echoing this chapter's insistence on impartial justice.
Expands the sabbatical year introduced here into the fuller Sabbath and Jubilee system.

study.oneStory: God demands courts that never bend, whether for bribery or misplaced pity, and He builds rest and gratitude directly into Israel's yearly rhythm through Sabbath and feast. This vision of uncompromising justice and joyful, provided-for rest runs through the whole Bible and comes to rest in Christ, our Passover and our firstfruits, who will gather in the final harvest of every nation.

quiz.q: What two opposite failures of justice does this chapter warn against in verses 2 to 3?

options.text: Following the crowd to pervert justice, and showing favoritism to a poor man in his lawsuit.
options.note: The text warns against bending justice in either direction, toward the majority or toward sympathy for the poor.

options.text: Only accepting bribes from the wealthy.
options.note: Bribery is addressed separately in verse 8; verses 2 to 3 specifically warn against crowd pressure and misplaced favoritism toward the poor.

options.text: Only ignoring the testimony of witnesses altogether.
options.note: The concern is bias in judgment, not simply ignoring testimony.

options.text: No warning about justice appears in this section.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 give explicit warnings about both forms of bias.

quiz.q: What must Israelites do if they find their enemy's ox or donkey straying, according to verse 4?

options.text: Return it to him.
options.note: The command explicitly requires practical kindness even toward an enemy's property.

options.text: Keep it as compensation for past wrongs.
options.note: The text requires returning the animal, not keeping it as compensation.

options.text: Report the enemy to the judges instead.
options.note: The command is simple and direct: return the animal personally.

options.text: No instruction is given regarding an enemy's stray animal.
options.note: Verse 4 gives an explicit instruction to return it.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of letting the land rest every seventh year, according to verse 11?

options.text: So that the poor among the people could eat from the field and wild animals could consume what was left.
options.note: This social and ecological purpose is stated directly in the text.

options.text: Because the soil would otherwise become permanently infertile.
options.note: The stated reason is provision for the poor and wildlife, not a claim about permanent soil damage.

options.text: To punish landowners for prior wrongdoing.
options.note: The rest is a built-in provision for the poor, not a punitive measure.

options.text: No reason is given for the sabbatical year.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly states the reason.

quiz.q: How many times a year were all Israel's males required to appear before the LORD, according to verse 17?

options.text: Three times a year.
options.note: The three feasts, Unleavened Bread, Harvest, and Ingathering, are explicitly named as the occasions.

options.text: Once a year, at Passover only.
options.note: The text specifies three occasions, not one.

options.text: Every single week.
options.note: Weekly Sabbath rest is commanded separately from these three annual pilgrimage feasts.

options.text: No fixed number is given.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly states three times a year.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament connect Christ to the Feast of Harvest introduced in this chapter?

options.text: Paul calls Christ's resurrection the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:20 draws this connection directly, echoing the firstfruits language of the Feast of Harvest.

options.text: The New Testament makes no connection between Christ and any Old Testament feast.
options.note: Paul explicitly ties Christ's resurrection to firstfruits, and calls Him the Passover lamb, 1 Corinthians 5:7 and 15:20.

options.text: Jesus explicitly abolished the Feast of Harvest as meaningless.
options.note: The New Testament fulfills the feast's meaning in Christ rather than declaring it meaningless.

options.text: Only the Feast of Unleavened Bread is ever connected to Christ.
options.note: Both the Passover and the Feast of Harvest are explicitly connected to Christ in Paul's writing.

questions: This law warns against both bribery and misplaced sympathy corrupting justice. Where in your own judgments of people or situations do you notice bias creeping in from either direction, favoring the powerful or excusing the sympathetic?
Israel was commanded to help even an enemy's struggling animal. Is there someone who has wronged you that God is calling you to practically help this week, regardless of the relationship between you?

The sabbatical year built rest for the poor directly into the economic calendar, not as charity alone but as structural provision. What structures in your own life or community could better build in rest and provision for those with less?
Three times a year, Israel's whole life paused for grateful worship before God. How intentional is the rhythm of gratitude in your own calendar, or does worship only happen when it is convenient?

This chapter insists God's people be marked by both uncompromising justice and structured generosity. Which of these two, justice or generosity, comes more naturally to you, and which do you need to grow in?

translationNotes.note: Do not show favoritism to a poor man in his lawsuit balances the frequent biblical concern for the poor by insisting that a judge's role is to determine truth impartially, not to rule based on social sympathy.

The Feast of Harvest, later called the Feast of Weeks or Pentecost, and the Feast of Ingathering, later called Tabernacles or Booths, are given brief mention here and expanded in fuller detail in Leviticus 23.

You must not cook a young goat in its mother's milk is a much-discussed command; interpreters have proposed it forbids a specific pagan fertility ritual, or more broadly guards a boundary between the giving of life, milk, and the taking of it.
